about.title: A Bit About Me
about.sub: From shipping games to shipping insights.
about.pivot_text: I spent years as a mobile game developer at studios like Zitga Studio and Inwave Studio — working across gameplay code, live-ops, and player-retention metrics, and shipping 5 games to Google Play. Chasing those retention numbers is what pulled me toward data: I wanted to understand the "why" behind the charts, not just watch them move. So I switched lanes into data analysis full-time.
about.think_text: I approach problems the way I designed game systems — find the feedback loop, measure what changes behaviour, cut what doesn't move the needle. Theory of Fun put words to it: learning IS the fun. Data analysis is the same thing with a different dataset.

p1.title: Global Tech Recruit Analysis
p1.desc: I cleaned 1.6M job postings end-to-end, built a gradient-boosted regression model (R² 0.53) to quantify how much seniority, role, skills and location actually drive salary.

p2.title: Cookie Cats A/B Testing
p2.desc: Does moving a game’s gate change who comes back? I dug into 90K players to find out — and the stats agree.

p3.title: E-Commerce Segmentation

footer.data: Data sourced from Google Jobs, Cookie Cats (Kaggle), UCI Online Retail II

p1.sub: End-to-end data pipeline connecting MotherDuck cloud warehouse via DuckDB, cleaning 1.6M job postings, producing 12 Plotly visualisations, and training a gradient-boosted regression model — R² 0.53, $22K MAE predicting US salary from seniority, role, skills and state.
p1.s1_title: Which roles actually pay the most?
p1.s1_sub: Median annual salary by job title — US market, 2023–2025
p1.s2_title: Is tech still hiring — and who’s going remote?
p1.s2_sub: Monthly job postings volume and remote-work share, Jan 2023 – Nov 2024
p1.s3_title: Which skills (and countries) are in demand?
p1.s3_sub: Top in-demand skills and leading countries by posting volume
p1.s1_insight: Senior Data Scientists and Engineers pull $142K–$150K median — nearly double a Data Analyst’s $82.5K. Sr. Data Engineers also lead remote at 14.4%, so seniority buys both money and flexibility.
p1.s2_insight: Hiring surged back to 84K postings in Feb 2025, confirming the market is active again. Remote share slid from a 13.6% peak in mid-2024 to ~4% now — those remote seats are competitive.
p1.s3_insight: SQL appears in 385K postings — the non-negotiable foundation of every data role. Demand spans 160 countries, so a distributed team is a real option if you’re location-flexible.
p1.s4_title: What do skills actually predict about salary?
p1.s4_sub: Gradient-boosted regression · US annual salary from seniority, role, skills & state
p1.chart_features_sub: Permutation importance · green = higher pay, red = lower
p1.s4_insight: Restricting to US postings and parsing a granular seniority level from the raw job title lifts the model to R² = 0.53 and MAE ≈ $22K — 39% better than always guessing the mean. Seniority is by far the biggest lever, followed by whether the role is an analyst (lower pay) and being based in California. The remaining variance reflects years of experience and the specific employer — not captured in this dataset.
p1.ml_note: How it works: A gradient-boosted model (HistGradientBoosting, which beat Linear, Ridge and Random Forest in a 5-fold CV bake-off) is trained on 79 features from US postings only — binary skill flags (SQL, Python, Spark…), how many skills each posting lists, an ordinal seniority level parsed from the raw job title, the job role, the top-10 hiring states, and a few extras (remote, health insurance, degree-optional). Permutation importance measures how much R² drops when each feature is shuffled. Green bars push a posting toward a higher salary; red toward lower. R² = 0.53 means these features explain a little over half of US salary variance — the rest is structurally invisible because exact years of experience, the specific employer, and total comp/equity are not columns in this dataset.
p1.s5_title: So what should you actually do?
p1.s5_sub: Data-driven moves to maximise earning potential
recs_items: Master SQL + Python first — they unlock every higher-paying role in the dataset.
Add Spark or AWS/Azure to your stack — they’re the single strongest salary signal in the model.
Aim for a Senior or Engineer title — seniority is the #1 predictor of landing above the $110K median.
Target remote-friendly US roles — they concentrate the highest compensation in the dataset.

p2.sub: Analysed an A/B test from the mobile game Cookie Cats (90,189 players) to determine whether moving the first gate from level 30 to level 40 affects player retention. Applied chi-square tests and bootstrap analysis to validate results.
p2.s1_title: How was the experiment set up?
p2.s1_sub: Random assignment of players into two groups
p2.s2_title: Does gate placement change who comes back?
p2.s2_sub: 1-day and 7-day retention rates by gate position
p2.s3_title: Is the difference real, or just luck?
p2.s3_sub: 1,000 bootstrap iterations — distribution of retention difference
p2.s4_title: Does the gate change how much people play?
p2.s4_sub: Total rounds played per group — player engagement patterns
p2.s1_insight: 44,700 players in Gate 30, 45,489 in Gate 40 — a near-perfect split that eliminates sampling as an explanation for any difference found.
p2.s2_insight: Gate 30 wins on 7-day retention: 19.02% vs 18.20%, and the gap is statistically significant (p = 0.0016). The 1-day gap is smaller and doesn’t clear the significance bar.
p2.s3_insight: The entire bootstrap distribution clears zero — the 95% CI is [+0.34%, +1.28%]. Not noise. Gate 30 consistently outperforms Gate 40 across 1,000 resamples.
p2.note_retention: Reading these bars: each is the return rate within its own group (players who came back ÷ that group’s size), so the two don’t add to 100% — they’re independent rates being compared, not a shared split. Whether the gap is real is tested with a chi-square test on the underlying player counts (a 2×2 table, df = 1): day-1 p = 0.0755 (not significant — a gap this size occurs ~7.5% of the time by chance), day-7 p = 0.0016 (significant — under ~0.2%).
p2.note_bootstrap: What the bootstrap does: the Gate 30 − Gate 40 difference is recomputed on thousands of resamples of the players (drawn with replacement), producing the distribution above. The 95% confidence interval [+0.34, +1.28 pp] is simply the middle 95% of that cloud. Because the whole interval sits to the right of the dashed zero line, the day-7 advantage holds up under resampling — it’s consistent, not an artefact of one random split.
p2.s4_insight: Both groups play roughly the same number of rounds — the gate doesn’t change how much people play, only whether they come back.
p2.conclusion_text: Keep the gate at level 30. The 7-day retention lift is statistically significant (p = 0.0016), confirmed by bootstrap, and comes at zero cost to engagement volume. Moving to level 40 only loses players long-term.

p3.sub: Analysed 541,909 transactions from a UK-based online retailer to segment 4,372 customers using RFM analysis, build cohort retention heatmaps, and generate actionable business recommendations for improving customer lifetime value.
p3.s1_title: Where does the revenue come from?
p3.s1_sub: Monthly revenue trend and geographic distribution
p3.s2_sub: Customer segments based on Recency, Frequency, and Monetary value
p3.s3_sub: Monthly cohort retention rates — tracking customer return behaviour
p3.chart_cohort_sub: % of customers returning each month after first purchase
p3.s4_title: So what should the business do next?
p3.s4_sub: Data-driven strategies to improve customer retention and revenue
p3.s1_insight: Revenue spikes hard in Q4 — November alone hit $1.46M. The UK drives 82% of total revenue, a concentration risk that one bad market quarter could hit hard.
p3.s2_insight: Champions (14.5% of customers) average $6,820 revenue vs $310 for Lost — a 22× gap. The 1,040 Hibernating customers are the highest-leverage win-back target right now.
p3.s3_insight: The Dec 2010 cohort retains at 36–41% monthly — every cohort after drops off noticeably. Holiday buyers are inherently higher-intent; the goal is recreating that acquisition quality outside Q4.
recs_items: Lock in your Champions (632 customers) with a loyalty tier — they’re 14.5% of your base but your highest-revenue segment.
Run a win-back campaign on 593 At-Risk customers now — personalised offers before they go fully dormant.
Grow Netherlands and Germany — strong demand, but together they’re only ~5.8% of revenue. Localised marketing is a cheap unlock.
Reverse-engineer the Dec 2010 cohort — 36–41% monthly retention is exceptional. Find out what made them stick and replicate it.
